summary: DHH argues that browser compatibility requirements are not a cost-free decision, contrary to what many clients assume. Working on a JavaScript-heavy web chat application, he describes the painful reality of cross-browser testing across platforms and versions since Netscape 4. He advocates educating customers that broad browser support is expensive, and questions whether clients would still demand it if they understood it could double or triple development costs for a small percentage of users on obscure browsers.
key_points: Clients mistakenly treat browser requirements as a cost-free choice, like picking a shirt color
Cross-browser JavaScript and HTML bugs make compatibility testing a tedious trial-and-error process
Developers must educate customers that browser compatibility is expensive in practice
The cost-benefit math rarely justifies supporting arcane browsers used by a small percentage of users
money_quotes: Some where along the lines customers who want to have web sites/application build got the impression that browser requirements was like choosing which color of shirt to wear today. A cost-free proposition. It's not.
Keeping track of all the different bugs in the JavaScript and HTML implementations in all browsers since Netscape 4 on Mac and Windows is pretty much impossible.
That's why we have to educate our customers that browser compatibility in this imperfect world is an expensive wish.

I wonder how many customers are really that concerned about shafting the small procentage of potential users who uses an arcane browser if it means they have to pay twice or thrice as much for the solution.
key_insight: Browser compatibility is an expensive engineering decision that clients wrongly treat as free, and developers must push back by making the true cost visible.
